retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Placement of an intracavitary applicator for an intact cervix
retrieved_docs.content: Proper applicator placement should be confirmed with plain radiography or more advanced imaging such as a CT scan or MRI. Adequate placement includes the tandem bisecting the ring/ovoids on AP and lateral imaging, the tandem being one-third to one-half the distance between the sacral promontory and the pubic symphysis, the tip of the tandem being below the sacral promontory, no packing located superior to the ring/ovoids, and no inferior displacement of the ring/ovoids relative to the flange.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: External devices use brackets or circum-zygomatic wires that are brought out percutaneously through the anterior cheek skin. These are joined to an external halo device, which is firmly anchored percutaneously to the temporoparietal skull. With the devices attached, a test should be performed to ensure no impingement is present, and the mobile midface does indeed move. The consolidation period is five days, and the rate of distraction is 1 mm/day. Internal devices are used for LeFort 1 advancement almost exclusively, and the consolidation phase lasts eight weeks, at which point the devices can be removed.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: External devices rely on titanium pins or wires that are implanted percutaneously to both the proximal and distal bone segments, and the distractor device attaches externally to the skin to these implanted pins. These also exist as both linear and multi-vector devices. The external devices were developed first, and so have the longest-term outcomes data available regarding their use. Some surgeons feel the distraction forces are not as efficiently transmitted with external devices, with some force transmission being lost due to flexion of the pins. External distraction can facilitate multi-vector distraction and allow for correction of asymmetries by adjusting the distraction vectors during the treatment period, which is not possible with implanted devices. Once consolidation is complete, the pins are removed in a minor procedure; a second open surgery is not required.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Placement of an intracavitary applicator for an intact cervix
retrieved_docs.content: Paraxial and para-coronal images should be obtained concerning the cervix-uteri and sagittal images. [66] These images can also allow for the assessment of chemoradiation response.  Technically, low- and high-intensity magnetic field MRI machines can be used. Patients can be given glucagon before MRI to reduce bowel motion. A pelvic coil is recommended to increase the signal-to-noise ratio. Ideally, a 3-mm slice thickness should be obtained, which helps improve the detection of parametrial involvement, but a slice thickness of less than 5mm is acceptable. The disadvantages of this approach are the increased costs, longer procedure times, and the need for strict compatibility of the materials utilized.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating the fetal position is another crucial component of intrapartum care and management. Several studies have shown increased adverse outcomes when the fetus is in an occiput posterior position, including a more prolonged second stage of labor and increased cesarean delivery rate. Randomized trials have shown that manual rotation of the fetus from an occiput posterior position to an occiput anterior position can reduce the length of the second stage of labor. [22] Manual rotation is more effective than maternal repositioning in labor, and it involves less technical skill and less risk than instrumental rotation. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Invagination -- Treatment / Management -- Evolution of Surgical Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: Sekir advocated the use of traction. For the few patients without any neurological disturbances, clinical and radiological preoperative traction for the progression of the disease has been described as a reasonable alternative to operative stabilization. [38] [39] The determination of the approach is usually a result of the reducibility of the BI. Given the importance of reducibility in the surgical approach, preoperative traction is a valuable tool in determining the degree of reducibility and assessment of neurological status. In a case series of surgically treated patients with BI performed by Goel et al., 82 patients without any associated Chiari malformation were placed in cervical traction, and 82% of these patients noted rapid clinical improvement after the application of traction. Traction can be a useful tool in evaluating the reducibility and predicting intraoperative neurological worsening by surgical position. Headframe reduction technique can be applied in patients with reducible atlantoaxial dislocation (AAD) and BI. [40] Irreducibility is defined as nonalignment of C1–2 during neck extension (determined on lateral x-ray) or after cervical traction. [28] Simple posterior fixation and fusion are adequate for reducible CVJ deformity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative techniques that encompasses several specific procedures. Among these, the Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or biologic substances to treat disease directly at the surgical site. The Delivery of vertex presentation describes an obstetric maneuver used during childbirth to assist the passage of a fetus presenting head‑first, often requiring careful manual handling to ensure safe delivery. Manual conversion of position denotes a technique in which a clinician manually repositions a patient’s body part or the entire patient to achieve optimal anatomical alignment for subsequent treatment or to correct malposition. Together, these three child procedures illustrate the diverse ways in which surgical manipulation can be employed to administer therapy, facilitate delivery, and adjust anatomical positioning, all of which are integral to effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps is a obstetric procedure in which the fetal head serves as the direct site of the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 893
retrieved_docs.content: بضع الارتفاق. “Other procedures associated with delivery” serves as a parent of the “other procedures to assist delivery” category and of the repair of episiotomy, illustrating how various ancillary obstetric techniques are hierarchically linked. Collectively, these relationships depict a structured hierarchy in obstetric care that integrates therapeutic‑agent applications, manual and instrument‑assisted fetal‑head manipulations, uterine versions, and multiple labor‑induction strategies under the overarching concepts of Surgical Manipulation and بضع الارتفاق, clarifying their clinical context and interconnections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: positioning methods, and a spectrum of complications—including cervical dystocia, uterine atony, prolonged or stimulated labor, and pelvic‑malformation‑related difficult deliveries—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding labor‑related care and its associated indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps and manual rotation of fetal head are obstetric interventions that are performed directly on the fetal head. Both procedures target the fetal head as the site of action, with delivery by double application of forceps used to assist extraction of the baby and manual rotation of fetal head employed to correct malposition. Manual rotation of fetal head is also described as a method of التداول, indicating that it is a technique used to rotate the fetal head into a more favorable orientation during labor. These interconnected procedures are important tools for managing difficult deliveries and optimizing outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent, Combined obstetrical version, and Manual conversion of position are all specific types of surgical manipulation. As child procedures of the broader category of surgical manipulation, each involves an operative technique aimed at altering a physiological condition during a medical intervention. Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication to achieve a therapeutic effect directly at the target site. Combined obstetrical version denotes a coordinated obstetric maneuver performed surgically to reposition a fetus to a more favorable presentation for delivery. Manual conversion of position describes a hands‑on technique used during surgery to adjust the patient’s or organ’s orientation to improve access or outcomes. Recognizing these procedures as subcategories of surgical manipulation helps clinicians select appropriate operative strategies, anticipate procedural requirements, and optimize patient care through targeted therapeutic and positional interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: with “Procedure on organ” also serving as an inverse‑ISA of fetal monitoring, thereby integrating organ‑based procedures within the fetal monitoring framework. This comprehensive, multilingual network clarifies how preventive, diagnostic, therapeutic and operative techniques—ranging from fetal monitoring and chorionic villus sampling to complex version maneuvers, forceps delivery, cervical cerclage, ectopic pregnancy surgery and hysterectomy—are classified, interrelated, and applied in obstetric patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: The community structures obstetric and gynecologic conditions and interventions in a hierarchical taxonomy that links pelvic abnormalities, placental operations, surgical manipulation, delivery techniques, cervical interventions, and termination methods to specific anatomical sites and clinical purposes. Pelvic abnormalities affecting pregnancy—such as uterine scars from prior surgery and obstetric lacerations—are recorded as findings in the uterus, with repair of a current obstetric laceration classified under OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS and as a child of OBSTETRICS → SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS. Ante‑partum obstetric procedures are grouped under operation on the placenta, which is also labeled a procedure in the uterus (عملية في الرحم). The parent concept Surgical Manipulation encompasses therapeutic‑agent application, combined obstetrical version, delivery by double forceps, delivery of a vertex presentation, external obstetrical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: open and laparoscopic management of ectopic gestations. Incision‑action is also associated with the placement of a suprapubic catheter (تركيب قسطرة فوق العانة) and with the reopening of a facial bone osteotomy site, the latter being synonymous with other therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system and classified under Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system. Overall, the system integrates procedural categories (Antepartum application, Pregnancy‑Related Procedures, Obstetrics incision) with specific techniques, interchangeable synonyms, and anatomical sites (female perineal structure, musculoskeletal system) to support comprehensive clinical planning, documentation, and patient care across obstetric, gynecologic, and musculoskeletal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Internal obstetrical version and manual conversion of position are both specific procedures classified as surgical manipulation techniques. Each of these methods is considered a child procedure within the broader category of surgical manipulation, reflecting their role as specialized interventions used to alter fetal position during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2124
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is classified as an other delivery procedure, placing it within the broader category of delivery interventions used in obstetric care to manage fetal positioning and facilitate safe childbirth.
